coredata.dc:title: Z-scheme heterojunction g-C3N4@PDA/BiOBr with biomimetic polydopamine as electron transfer mediators for enhanced visible-light driven degradation of sulfamethoxazole
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Furong
Chen, Jichong
Zhao, Jingzheng
Chen, Zhuo
Xia, Dongsheng
Zhan, Zhilan
Wang, Qiang
coredata.dc:description:
Visible-light photocatalysis boosted by Z-scheme photocatalysts has been deemed as an economic and effective way in eliminating emerging antibiotics pollution. A Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst g-C3N4@PDA/BiOBr (CNPB) was hereby facilely constructed through polydopamine (PDA) modification of g-C3N4 followed by deposition of BiOBr component via a solvothermal reaction. The structure, morphology, optical and photoelectrochemical properties etc. of CNPB were characterized and explored. The photocatalytic activity of CNPB was assessed on the degradation of sulfamethoxazole (SMX). CNPB manifested remarkably improved photocatalytic performance than the as-prepared g-C3N4, BiOBr and g-C3N4/BiOBr heterojunction in the degradation of SMX under visible light irradiation. SMX could be nearly completely degraded in 60 mins by CNPB, while the degradation rate of SMX under g-C3N4, BiOBr, g-C3N4/BiOBr heterojunction, and the “g-C3N4@PDA + BiOBr” mixture were 30%, 42%, 71%, and 47%, respectively. The highly enhanced photocatalytic efficiency of CNPB was attributed to the synergistic effect of improved light harvesting and accelerated transport and separation of photo-generated carriers in the Z-scheme structure with PDA as efficient electron transfer mediators. A Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism was confirmed by experiments on radical scavenging and electron spin resonance (ESR) signals trapping, which demonstrated that the h+ and
O2 − were the major reactive species in oxidizing SMX. The degradation mechanism, main intermediates and reaction pathways were further explored. Besides, CNPB exhibited good stability and reusability in multiple uses. The construction of Z-scheme photocatalyst using biomimetic polydopamine as electron transfer mediators may provide an insight into designing new and efficient photocatalysts for environmental applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Visible-light photocatalysis boosted by Z-scheme photocatalysts has been deemed as an economic and effective way in eliminating emerging antibiotics pollution. A Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst g-C3N4@PDA/BiOBr (CNPB) was hereby facilely constructed through polydopamine (PDA) modification of g-C3N4 followed by deposition of BiOBr component via a solvothermal reaction. The structure, morphology, optical and photoelectrochemical properties etc. of CNPB were characterized and explored. The photocatalytic activity of CNPB was assessed on the degradation of sulfamethoxazole (SMX). CNPB manifested remarkably improved photocatalytic performance than the as-prepared g-C3N4, BiOBr and g-C3N4/BiOBr heterojunction in the degradation of SMX under visible light irradiation. SMX could be nearly completely degraded in 60 mins by CNPB, while the degradation rate of SMX under g-C3N4, BiOBr, g-C3N4/BiOBr heterojunction, and the “g-C3N4@PDA + BiOBr” mixture were 30%, 42%, 71%, and 47%, respectively. The highly enhanced photocatalytic efficiency of CNPB was attributed to the synergistic effect of improved light harvesting and accelerated transport and separation of photo-generated carriers in the Z-scheme structure with PDA as efficient electron transfer mediators. A Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism was confirmed by experiments on radical scavenging and electron spin resonance (ESR) signals trapping, which demonstrated that the h+ and
O2 − were the major reactive species in oxidizing SMX. The degradation mechanism, main intermediates and reaction pathways were further explored. Besides, CNPB exhibited good stability and reusability in multiple uses. The construction of Z-scheme photocatalyst using biomimetic polydopamine as electron transfer mediators may provide an insight into designing new and efficient photocatalysts for environmental applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the ever increased use of antibiotics in the past few decades [1], the side effects of antibiotics have obtained high concern because of their potential hazard to the microbial communities, ecosystem and even human health [2–4]. As one of the commonly used sulfonamide antibiotics, sulfamethoxazole (SMX) has been widely found in the effluents of municipal sewage treatment plants, surface water, and even in drinking water [2,5–7]. Since the conventional wastewater treatment approaches via biological, chemical and adsorption methods etc. are not satisfactory in effectively removing SMX residues [2,3,6,7], the search of highly efficient elimination methods is in urgent demanding. An array of advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) has been investigated [1–3,6]. Among these methods, semiconductor-based visible light photocatalysis has received even higher interest as it is a clean, efficient, economic and safe technology, which only use the solar lights as driving power and appropriate semiconductors as catalysts to perform oxidation reactions for the decomposition of different types of pollutants through the reactive species sourced from photo-induced electrons and holes [2,8–10].

However, the use of single component semiconductor often suffers from easy recombination of the photo-induced electrons and holes and lower visible light harvesting efficiency. Construction of heterojunction has proven to be an efficacious approach to heighten the photocatalytic activity of semiconductors due to the improved charge carriers separation [11,12]. Nevertheless, it is normally hard to achieve efficient charge separation and strong redox capability simultaneously on the conventional type of heterojunctions [11,12]. In this regard, Z-scheme heterojunctions have emerged as a better solution because their special band structures enable electrons transfer from one semiconductor holding more positive CB potential to the other semiconductor with more negative VB potential, thus retaining electrons in more negative CB and holes in more positive VB. This structure possesses holes with powerful oxidative capability in the lower VB and electrons with strong reductive ability in the higher CB of the two different semiconductors. Consequently, the recombination between electrons and holes is inhibited whereas high redox capability is retained in the Z-scheme structures [11–13].

In recent years, the 2-D structure and environmentally friendly graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has received high attention since it exhibits potential application in visible light photocatalysis due to its attractive advantages such as tunable electronic structure, suitable band energy (~2.7 eV), high stability, and easy preparation etc [7,12,13]. However, the activity of single component g-C3N4 is still limited by fast recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, weak electrical conductivity, and lacked absorption of the lights with wavelength higher than 460 nm [14]. Many efforts have been endeavored to improve the visible-light photocatalytic efficiency of g-C3N4, such as elemental doping, combination with other semiconductors or carbonaceous materials, and structure modification through electron-acceptor coupling [2,11,13–16]. On the other hand, among many of the bismuth-based photocatalysts, BiOBr is more promising due to its appropriate band energy, layered structure, visible light response, and environmental benign etc. However, the rapid recombination of photo-induced charge carrier also retarded the application of single component BiOBr [12,17].

Because of the matchable layered structure features and band energies between g-C3N4 and BiOBr, many Z-scheme heterojunctions constructed with BiOBr and g-C3N4 have shown enhanced photocatalytic performance [17–20]. However, direct Z-scheme is often not sufficiently effective in promoting charge separation due to weak electron transport [21,22]. Nevertheless, Z-scheme photocatalysts with an electron transfer mediator bridging the two semiconductors can further enhance the performance as the transfer of photo-induced change carriers and their separation will be accelerated [11,21,23,24]. Because of their conjugated π structure, electron transport ability and unique optical properties, carbonaceous nanomaterials (carbon nanotubes, carbon dots, rGO etc.) have recently been employed as electron transfer mediators in the g-C3N4/BiOBr Z-scheme heterojunctions to effectively improve their photocatalytic activity based on the synergistic effect of enhanced light harvesting and charge carriers separation [13,15]. For example, the BiOBr/CDs/g-C3N4 Z-scheme heterojunction with carbon dots as an electron mediator exhibited strengthened efficiency for the degradation of ciprofloxacin and tetracycline under visible light [15]. The use of carbon dots also enabled g-C3N4 nanosheets/CDs/BiOBr Z-scheme high performance in visible light driven degradation of organic dyes and photoreduction of Cr(VI) [25]. With the use of rGO as an electron transfer mediator, both Z-Scheme photocatalysts of BiOBr/rGO/protonated g-C3N4 [12] and BiOCl/rGO/protonated g-C3N4 [26] exhibited excellent performance in the degradation of tetracycline and RhB. g-C3N4/MCNTs/BiOI Z-scheme nanocomposite also showed improved visible-light photocatalytic activities by using multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MCNTs) as an electron mediator [13].

The mussel-inspired biomimetic carbon materials polydopamine (PDA) also possesses conjugated π structure and good electron transport ability, and it has attracted interests in various areas due to its excellent adhesion ability, strong light-harvesting capacity, photoconductivity, and biocompatibility [14,27]. The π-π* electron delocalization between the π-π conjugated structure of g-C3N4 and PDA can yield a synergism and boost the charge carriers transfer [28–31]. PDA/g-C3N4 composites have been reported exhibiting heightened photocatalytic efficiency in organics degradation and H2 evolution under visible light [14,27], due to the improved light harvesting and efficient separation and rapid transportation of photogenerated charge carriers based on the PDA modification [29]. The use of PDA as electron transfer mediators has significantly improved the photocatalytic activities of the composites including Ag/AgCl-PDA-g-C3N4 [32], BiVO4/PDA/g-C3N4 [33], SiO2@PDA@BiOBr [34], and Ag/PDA/g-C3N4 [35].

Inspired by Z-scheme heterojunction systems constructed with g-C3N4 and BiOBr using the carbonaceous nanomaterials, such as carbon nanotubes, carbon dots and rGO, as electron transfer mediators, we envisage that the Z-scheme of g-C3N4@PDA/BiOBr photocatalyst incorporating PDA as electron transfer mediators might also result in excellent photocatalytic activity. Compared with carbon nanotubes, carbon dots or rGO, PDA is highly biocompatible and much cheaper and facile to fabricate on the surface of various materials. Large quantities of functional groups in the PDA structure, such as primary amine, tertiary amine and catechol, could also act as the anchors for metal ions [29] to facilitate the close interfacial interactions between g-C3N4, PDA and BiOBr, thus benefitting the transfer of photo-induced charges within the ternary g-C3N4@PDA/BiOBr structure [12,21].

However, as far as we know, there is no report until now on the Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst using PDA as electron transfer mediators bridging g-C3N4 and BiOBr. Herein, the main aim of this work was to construct and study the photocatalytic activity of a novel g-C3N4@PDA/BiOBr Z-scheme. SMX was used as a model pollutant to evaluate the catalytic performance of g-C3N4@PDA/BiOBr under visible light. The influence of several factors including pH and water matrices etc. was investigated. The mechanism and plausible pathways of photocatalytic degradation of SMX by g-C3N4@PDA/BiOBr were further elucidated.

g-C3N4 was prepared by pyrolyzing 10 g of melamine in a muffle furnace. After calcination at 550 °C for 3 h with a heating rate of 5 °C/min, the obtained yellow product was cooled naturally before being ground into fine powder [14].

0.25 g of g-C3N4 was added into a Tris-HCl buffer solution (25 mL, 10 mM, pH 8.5), and the mixture was sonicated for 20 min. Under vigorous stirring, 0.062 g of dopamine hydrochloride (Da·HCl) was then slowly added to the mixture, followed by adjusting pH to 8.5 with 1 M NaOH (aq). The mixture was continuously stirred for 24 h at ambient conditions [14]. It was then centrifuged and the collected samples of g-C3N4@PDA were washed with ultrapure water and methanol, respectively, followed by drying under vacuum at 40 °C. The mass ratio of g-C3N4 to the theoretical amount of PDA (calculated approximately from the amount of DA precursor) was 1:0.2, while other samples with different mass ratios of g-C3N4:PDA as 1:0.05, 1:0.1 and 1:0.3 were also prepared by changing the relative amount of DA precursor. It should be pointed out that the photocatalyst based on g-C3N4:PDA = 1:0.2 was mostly utilized in this work as it displayed the highest performance, and it was denoted as g-C3N4@PDA for simplicity unless otherwise stated.

Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (0.16 g, 0.33 mmol) was dissolved in a solvent containing 16 mL of ethylene glycol and 2 mL of H2O, followed by addition of 0.1 g g-C3N4@PDA. The mixture was sonicated for 10 min before addition of 0.3 g PVP dissolved in 10 mL of ethylene glycol. The mixture was vigorously stirred for 30 min, and KBr (0.04 g, 0.33 mmol) in 2 mL of ethylene glycol was slowly dropped into the mixture under agitation. After stirring further for 30 min, the mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave (40 mL) and kept at 120 °C for 5 h and then cooled to room temperature. The product was collected by centrifugation, washed with ultrapure water and methanol, respectively, before being dried at 60 °C for 3 h. The mass ratio of g-C3N4@PDA to the theoretical amount of BiOBr yielded from this preparation was 1:1, while other samples with different mass ratios of g-C3N4@PDA:BiOBr as 1:0.5 and 1:2 were also prepared similarly by changing the amount of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and KBr relative to g-C3N4@PDA. It should be pointed out that the photocatalyst g-C3N4@PDA/BiOBr with the mass ratio of g-C3N4@PDA:BiOBr = 1:1 was mostly utilized in this work as it was found possessing the best performance, and it was represented as g-C3N4@PDA/BiOBr (CNPB) for simplicity unless otherwise stated. The preparation of g-C3N4@PDA/BiOBr (CNPB) was summarized in Fig. 1 . The photos of the samples with distinct colour difference are presented in Fig. S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity evaluation of CNPB for the degradation of SMX
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4@PDA/BiOBr was evaluated on the degradation of SMX at room temperature under a simulated visible light irradiation using a 300 W Xe lamp (CEL-HXUV300, CEAULIGHT, Beijing) with a 400 nm cut-off filter (Fig. S2). Briefly, 30 mg of g-C3N4@PDA/BiOBr was added into 50 mL of aqueous solution with an initial SMX concentration of 2.5 ppm. The mixture was first stirred for 30 min in the dark to achieve a balanced adsorption/desorption process. The suspension was then subjected to the visible light irradiation under magnetic stirring. Certain volumes of the suspension was sampled at regular periods and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane, and the filtrates were analyzed by HPLC or UPLC-MS/MS. For the comparison experiments, the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4, BiOBr, g-C3N4@PDA, and a physical mixture of “g-C3N4@PDA + BiOBr” were tested respectively using the amount corresponding to that in the g-C3N4@PDA/BiOBr. For the stability and reusability evaluation, the catalyst was recovered by centrifugation after each reaction, washed with ultrapure water and methanol respectively for several times, and dried before being tested for the next reaction cycle.
The detailed information of Materials and reagents, Characterization methods, and Analytical methods are provided in the Supplementary Materials (Text S1–S4).

FT-IR spectra of the prepared samples are presented in Fig. 2 (a). The absorption bands at 1400, 1510 and 1624 cm−1 for PDA were assigned to the vibration of the indoline and indole groups and C-N stretching [14,32]. The wide range absorption from 3200 to 3500 cm−1 were assigned to –OH and –NH2 stretching vibrations, respectively [36]. The absorption at 815 cm−1 in the spectrum of g-C3N4 corresponded to the stretching of the triazine units. A series of typical absorptions in the region of 1200–1650 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching of C–N and CN in the heptazine motifs, while the broad absorptions within 3100 and 3400 cm−1 are assigned to the NH or NH2 residues from the inadequate condensation [14,15,37,38]. The absorption at 525 cm−1 in the spectrum of BiOBr was related to the Bi-O stretching [15,37], while the absorption around 1650 cm−1 was due to the stretching and bending vibrations of O–H form the surface water [37]. The spectrum of g-C3N4@PDA was very similar to that of g-C3N4, and this might be attributed to the overlap by the absorptions from g-C3N4 [32]. Although the absorptions overlaps also occurred in the spectra of CNPB, it was obvious that the characteristic absorptions of g-C3N4 and BiOBr could be found in the spectra of CNPB, indicating the structure feature of g-C3N4 and BiOBr was preserved in the heterojunction [37].

XRD pattern (Fig. 2(b)) of g-C3N4 exhibited a strong diffraction at 27.4° and a weak one at 13.0° corresponding to the (0 0 2) and (1 0 0) planes of a graphitic-like structure, respectively [12,15,37]. The XRD pattern of BiOBr was consistent with that in a tetragonal structure (JCPDS 73-2061), indicating a single phase of the as-prepared BiOBr sample [12,15,37]. The XRD pattern of PDA displayed a broad peak around 22° due to an amorphous structure of PDA [34]. The possible formation of bismuth oxide (Bi2O3) in BiOBr component and CNPB composite was excluded by comparing with the characteristic XRD diffraction patterns of Bi2O3 [39]. The characteristic peaks of g-C3N4 and BiOBr were observed in the diffractions of CNPB sample, indicating that the crystal phases of BiOBr and g-C3N4 were retained in the CNPB heterojunction. The mean primary crystallite size was estimated using Scherrer’s formula [40] and the crystallite size of single-component g-C3N4 and BiOBr was 7 nm and 29 nm, respectively. The crystallite size of g-C3N4 and BiOBr in the CNPB composite was found to be 7 nm and 15 nm, respectively (Table S1).

The microscopic morphologies of CNPB and its precursor components are shown in Fig. 3 . The SEM images of g-C3N4 showed mixed aggregation with block and some flaky structures, which was similar to that reported by Yu and co-workers [41]. In comparing with g-C3N4, the morphology of g-C3N4@PDA showed a relatively rougher surface, with a lot of dispersed sheet materials adhering to the block surface. Fig. 3 (d) showed an interface (indicated by red dot line) between g-C3N4 and PDA, wherein the PDA with a thickness of about 5–10 nm was bound to the edge of g-C3N4, confirming the successful modification of g-C3N4 by PDA [14]. Fig. 3 (c), (e) and (g) showed the loading of flower-like BiOBr microspheres on the surface of g-C3N4@PDA [7]. The TEM image in Fig. 3 (e) further confirmed that the flower-like structure of BiOBr was assembled from the loosely packed thin petals [42]. A thin layer of PDA on g-C3N4 with ~5–10 nm thickness could be observed from the enlarged TEM view of CNPB (Fig. S3). The close contact between g-C3N4@PDA and BiOBr shown in Fig. 3(c) and 3(e) would benefit the interfacial charge transmission [43,44]. The EDX spectrum (Fig. 3f) of CNPB indicated the elemental composition of C, N, O, Bi and Br [17]. The presence of PDA in CNPB was also confirmed by the EDX elemental results (Table S2), which showed a C/N atom ratio of ~1.3 that was higher than 0.75 for g-C3N4. EDS mappings showed that all of the five elements were found in the selected region of the CNPB composite, confirming the successful deposition of BiOBr on the surface of g-C3N4@PDA to form a ternary heterojunction of g-C3N4@PDA/BiOBr [15,25].

The specific surface area and pore data of the samples were measured with the results presented in Fig. 4 (a) and Table 1 , respectively. The nitrogen adsorption-desorption pattern of the samples could be assigned to type IV isotherms with a hysteresis loop, indicating the presence of mesoporous structures. Compared with g-C3N4, the surface area and pore volumes of g-C3N4@PDA were slightly reduced, implying that PDA modification of g-C3N4 might block some pores in the surface of g-C3N4. The fabrication of BiOBr on g-C3N4@PDA markedly increased the specific surface area of CNPB. It was seen from the SEM that the hierarchical flower-like BiOBr possessed large amount of pores on the surface [45], thus increasing the specific surface areas of CNPB.

The thermal analysis results obtained in the nitrogen atmosphere are displayed in Fig. 4(b). g-C3N4 was thermally stable below ca. 560 °C, while it could be fully decomposed when the temperature was higher than 750 °C. However, g-C3N4@PDA could not be completely decomposed when the temperature was above 750 °C, and the reason might be that PDA was carbonized to form carbon materials at high temperature [46,47]. TGA comparison between g-C3N4 and g-C3N4@PDA also confirmed the successful modification of g-C3N4 with PDA. Further comparison of the TGA patterns between CNPB, g-C3N4@PDA and BiOBr indicated that the relative weight ratio of BiOBr and g-C3N4@PDA was close to 1:1 in the CNPB composite as being designed.

The surface chemical compositions of CNPB were analyzed by XPS, and the results are shown in Fig. 5 . The spectra of wide survey in Fig. 5(a) revealed that the CNPB composite constituted of C, N, O, Bi and Br elements. C 1 s spectra in Fig. 5(b) could be deconvoluted into four peaks with the binding energies at 284.60, 285.35, 286.24 and 288.18 eV, respectively, corresponding to C–C, C–N, C–O and N–C–(N)2. The peak at 286.24 eV of C–O arising from PDA may confirm the presence of PDA in the CNPB composite [14,32,35]. Deconvolution of the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 5(c)) showed three peaks with binding energies at 398.51, 399.50 and 401.20 eV, respectively, corresponding to the sp2-bonded aromatic N linked to C (C–NC), tertiary N connected to C [N-(C)3], terminal amino groups (C–N–H), and the positively charged nitrogen atoms (–NH-+) [14,34]. XPS spectrum of Br 3d in the CNPB is shown in Fig. 5(d), with the peaks at 68.16 and 69.16 eV corresponding to Br 3d5/2 and Br 3d3/2, respectively [12,15]. The corresponding binding energies for Br 3d in the pure BiOBr were 67.92 and 68.95 eV, respectively. Deconvolution of O 1 s spectrum in Fig. 5(e) resulted in three peaks at 529.88, 531.38 and 533.08 eV, respectively, corresponding to Bi-O, surface –OH/H2O, and adsorbed CO2, respectively [12,14,15]. While the spectrum of pure BiOBr exhibited the peaks for the O 1 s of Bi-O and surface –OH/H2O at 529.63 and 531.00 eV, respectively. The XPS spectra of Bi 4f in CNPB in Fig. 5(f) showed two peaks at 159.06 and 164.36 eV, corresponding to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, respectively [12,15]. The two Bi 4f binding energies in pure BiOBr were centered at 158.81 and 164.13 eV, respectively. The XPS showed that the binding energies of Bi, O and Br elements associated with the BiOBr unit in the CNPB shifted slightly to higher binding energy compared with pure BiOBr, and this might be caused by the electron transfer from BiOBr to g-C3N4@PDA. Similar phenomena were also reported in literatures, implying an interface chemical interaction between BiOBr and g-C3N4@PDA [12,44,48–50].

The UV–vis diffuse reflection spectroscopies of the samples are shown in Fig. 6 (a). The absorption edge of g-C3N4 was 460 nm, while that of BiOBr was 440 nm, close to those reported in literature [12,15,37]. The absorption edge of g-C3N4/BiOBr composite prepared with the same amount ratio as in CNPB was only slightly larger than that of g-C3N4, indicating coupling BiOBr with g-C3N4 would not significantly extend the absorption range [15]. However, the DRS spectra of CNPB exhibited significant red-shift and strong visible light absorption, suggesting an increased ability of CNPB to harvest more visible lights after the introduction of PDA.

The band-gap energies were calculated by the Kubelka-Munk equations (Eq. (1)) [14,15].

n / 2
where α, h, ν, E g, and A represent the absorption coefficient, Planck constant, light frequency, band gap energy, and a constant. n values of the indirect transitions of g-C3N4 and BiOBr are 4 [15]. The plots based on Eq. (1) were thereby obtained as shown in Fig. 6(b). The band gap energies (E g) for g-C3N4 and BiOBr were calculated as 2.65 and 2.78 eV, respectively [12,15,37].

Besides the light adsorption ability, the generation and recombination of 0photo-induced electron-hole pairs, and the transfer of carriers, are vital factors influencing the photocatalyst’s activity [14]. Therefore, the photoluminescence (PL) spectra excited at 360 nm were conducted. Fig. 6(c) showed that g-C3N4 produced an intense emission at around 462 nm, indicating a fast recombination of photo-induced carriers. The PL intensity of g-C3N4/BiOBr was much lower than that of g-C3N4, suggesting formation of a heterojunction composite was effective to enhance the charge carriers separation [12]. After modification of PDA between g-C3N4 and BiOBr, CNPB exhibited the lowest PL emission, indicating significantly reduced charge recombination. This could be explained by the electron transfer function of PDA, arising from the extended π-conjugated system with abundant semiquinone and quinone groups [14,28]. Therefore, both increased light absorption and reduced electron-hole recombination could be achieved by the construction of CNPB, which would endow the CNPB composite with high photocatalytic activity.

Photoresponses of the samples were tested by photocurrent experiments to examine the charge separation efficiency. Fig. 7 (a) revealed that CNPB possessed the largest photocurrent value, indicating that the catalyst CNPB could efficiently extend the lifetime of the photo-induced electron-hole pairs and enhance the charge transport capacity [12,14,15]. Photocurrent responses were also measured in the negative potential (Fig. S4). It was found that the photocurrent reduced when SMX was added into the electrolyte, which might be attributed to the consumption of photo-induced electrons by SMX, implying the contribution of photo-induced electrons in the degradation process.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was also tested to reveal the mobility and separation of photogenerated charges [12,14,15]. EIS plots in Fig. 7(b) showed that CNPB displayed the smallest radius, indicating it had a relatively smaller charge transfer resistance which would afford a reinforced separation and fast transport of electrons and holes. The results from PL spectra and EIS plots confirmed the constructive function of PDA as a charge transfer mediator in the interface of g-C3N4 and BiOBr for enhancing electron transmission and reducing charge recombination.

The results in Fig. 8 showed that the removal of SMX was dominantly due to photodegradation as the adsorption of SMX by CNPB was relatively very small. Fig. 8 (a) showed the influence of different PDA proportions in the CNPB composite on the catalytic activity. When the relative mass ratio of PDA over g-C3N4 in g-C3N4@PDA was increased from 5% to 10% and 20%, respectively, the photocatalytic activity of CNPB gradually increased. However, when the relative mass ratio was further increased to 30%, the photocatalytic activity of CNPB decreased. Therefore, photocatalytic capability of CNPB was promoted by PDA modification of g-C3N4, and CNPB with a relative mass ratio of 20% PDA in the g-C3N4@PDA unit exhibited a highly efficient photocatalytic activity. PDA possessed an important function in improving the photocatalytic efficiency of CNPB. However, redundant PDA coating might obstruct the absorption and utilization of light irradiation [14]. Fig. 8 (b) showed the effect of different mass ratio of BiOBr relative to g-C3N4@PDA on the photocatalytic performance, among which the PDA ratio was fixed as 20% relative to g-C3N4. It was found that when the BiOBr loading ratio was increased from 0.5 to 1 over g-C3N4@PDA, the performance of CNPB was enhanced markedly. However, when the mass ratio was further increased to 2:1, the photocatalytic performance of CNPB obviously decreased. This can be explained by the reason that excessive BiOBr might cover the active sites of g-C3N4@PDA and hinder the light penetration to g-C3N4@PDA components.

The photocatalytic activity of CNPB with the optimal constitution ratio of g-C3N4, PDA and BiOBr was compared with other samples possessing relevant amount as their accounts in the CNPB, and the results are presented in Fig. 8(c). It was found that pure BiOBr and pure g-C3N4 exhibited relatively much lower performance. The degradation ratio of SMX upon 60 min visible light irradiation was 42% and 30%, respectively, under the catalysis of BiOBr and g-C3N4. The g-C3N4/BiOBr composite remarkably promoted the degradation efficiency and 71% of SMX could be degraded in 60 min by g-C3N4/BiOBr composite. This could be explained by the formation of heterojunction structure which favored the transport and separation of photo-induced charge carriers [37]. Nevertheless, the CNPB composite exhibited the highest activity among the tested samples, and SMX was nearly completely degraded in 60 min by CNPB under visible light irradiation. However, only 47% of the SMX was removed when a physical mixture of “g-C3N4@PDA + BiOBr” was used, which was much lower than CNPB. It was found that although the activity of PDA/BiOBr was slightly higher than BiOBr, the activity of g-C3N4@PDA was much smaller than g-C3N4, which was probably due to that the coating of PDA obstructed the absorption and light utilization by g-C3N4. This result also indicated a synergetic effect among g-C3N4, PDA and BiOBr [12], and suggested that the modification of PDA on g-C3N4 and further coupling with BiOBr constructed a Z-scheme heterojunction, which facilitated intimate interfacial interactions between two functional unit phases of g-C3N4@PDA and BiOBr [37]. The enhanced performance of CNPB could be explained by the reason that the introduction of PDA as electron transfer mediators facilitated faster interfacial charge transfer and more efficient separation of the photo-induced electrons and holes [14,15,34], which has been evidenced by the aforementioned PL and photoelectrochemical study.

HPLC chromatograms of the SMX solution at different degradation periods are depicted in Fig. 8(d). The peak with a retention time at 8.1 min corresponded to SMX. It decreased gradually during the reaction process and almost completely disappeared after the solution was irradiated for 60 min with visible light under the catalysis of CNPB. Several small peaks from the reaction intermediates were also observed in the HPLC plots. TOC was measured to get further information on the mineralization of SMX during the photocatalytic process. As shown in Fig. S5, the TOC removal rate was 35% and 60%, respectively, in 60 min and 180 min, indicating that CNPB could use visible light to mineralize SMX to various intermediates.

Initial solution pH is one of the major factors that affects the photocatalytic degradation of SMX, as it possesses a zwitterionic structure which is sensitive to pH variations [7]. Thus, the effect of various solution pH on the degradation of SMX was evaluated with the results shown in Fig. 9 (a). The point of zero charge (pHpzc) of CNPB was measured to be around 1.91 (Fig. S6), indicating the surface of CNPB was positively charged at pH < 1.91 and negatively charged at pH > 1.91. The pH of the control experiment without solution pH adjustment was about 6.5, and Fig. 9(a) showed that SMX could be nearly completely degraded after 60 min illumination. The degradation ratio of SMX at 60 min irradiation was 80% and 88%, respectively, under the acidic conditions with initial pH = 2 and 4. The catalyst CNPB tended to be aggregated when the solution pH was close to its pHpzc, causing reduced surface area and thus resulting in a lower SMX removal efficiency [51]. When the pH was increased to 8, the degradation efficiency decreased slightly to 93% after 60 min reaction compared to the control experiment. However, when the initial pH was further increased to more basic condition at pH = 10, degradation efficiency dropped markedly, and only 62% of SMX was degraded after reaction for 60 min. It was obvious that the SMX degradation efficiency improved along with the increase of initial pH from a stronger acidic to neutral medium, and then decreased at high alkaline pH condition [3,7,8]. SMX molecule possesses two pKa of 1.8 and 5.7, respectively [7,8], which means SMX molecule is mainly neutral in a solution with the pH range of 1.8–5.7, while the cationic form (SMX+) is the dominant species at pH < 1.8 and the anionic form (SMX−) is dominating at pH > 5.7. The neutral form of SMX has stronger light absorption and higher photochemical reactivity [52]. Under strong acidic conditions, SMX and CNPB were likely to be positively charged and this did not favor their interactions. Protons in the solution might also react with the
O2 − [6], resulting in the consumption of
O2 − and lower photo-degradation efficiency. On the other hand, SMX became negative at highly basic medium, and the repulsive effect between SMX− and the negatively charged CNPB would inhibit their interactions [8]. Besides,
OH was consumed to react with OH−, resulting lower degradation efficiency due to decreased
OH concentration [3]. Furthermore, CO2 produced from the degradation process could be converted to HCO3 − or CO3 2− under alkaline condition, which could consume the oxidizing species of h+ and
OH [37]. Nevertheless, SMX could still be effectively photo-degraded under CNPB when the initial solution pH lied in the range of 4–8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of some model matters in water matrix
ce:section.ce:para: Humic acid (HA) is an important component of natural organic matter and it commonly presents in the surface water and sewage treatment plants in the range of 0.1 to 10 mg/L [6,53]. Therefore, the influence of humic acid on the activity of CNPB was examined. Fig. 9 (b) showed that SMX degradation was slightly inhibited when the concentration of HA was increased. When HA concentration was increased to 5 mg/L, the photodegradation rate of SMX was reduced by 10% compared to the control experiment with no HA addition. The inhibition effect of HA might be due to that HA could compete with SMX for the reactive oxidizing species. HA could also compete with CNPB for the photons, resulting in a reduced production of reactive species in the solution [6]. Nevertheless, CNPB was still robust against the interference of HA, and 90% of SMX could be degraded under a relatively high HA concentration of 5 mg/L.

Bicarbonate (HCO3 −) is a typical inorganic species widely existed in natural water system with a mean concentration of 264 mg/L (~4.3 mM) in ground water [54], thus the effect of bicarbonate on the photo-degradation of SMX was also evaluated with the results as shown in Fig. 9 (c). It was found that the degradation efficiency of SMX decreased gradually when the concentration of HCO3 − was increased. When the HCO3 − concentration was increased from 0 to 0.5 and further to 5 mM, respectively, the SMX removal rate was decreased from 99.8% to 75% and further to 55%, respectively. This was due to that HCO3 − or CO3 2− could react with the photo-induced reactive species of h+ and
OH to produce carbonate radicals (CO3 −), which were less reactive than h+ and
OH [6,37,43]. It is of note that the SMX concentration (2.5 ppm) was higher than typical antibiotic concentrations in the water with a range of ng/L to μg/L [40]. The concentration of 2.5 ppm SMX has been generally adopted in literature to facilitate SMX quantitation and secure reliable analysis with the analytical protocols [2,6,40,55]. Nevertheless, the effects of HA (5 mg/L) and HCO3 − (5 mM) were evaluated for the degradation of SMX at lower concentrations of 20 ppb and 100 ppb, respectively. The results in Fig. S7 showed that SMX could still be effectively degraded, while the inhibiting effect of HCO3 − was much higher than HA similar to that observed for the SMX at a concentration of 2.5 ppm.

It is worth noting that the process parameters including pH, temperature and water matrixes may all affect to different extent for the ionization and hydrolysis of SMX, the surface properties and band structure of CNPB, and the formation of reactive species [56]. Therefore, the evaluation of types and extent of by-products formation during SMX degradation remains interesting and challenging [57]. The combination of density functional theory and transition state theory with experimental results may pave the way for this endeavor [57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and reusability evaluation of CNPB
ce:section.ce:para: In order to assess the potential application of CNPB, the photocatalytic stability and reusability of CNPB was evaluated. Fig. 9 (d) showed that 88% of SMX could still be degraded even after the CNPB was used repeatedly for five times. The slight decrease in the efficiency of CNPB might be caused by the accumulation of degradation intermediates on the surface of CNPB, thus blocking some of the active sites of the catalyst [8,40]. FT-IR, XRD, SEM and XPS results recorded for CNPB composite before and after photodegradation of SMX confirmed no apparent discrepancy of structure and phase for the recycled CNPB samples (Figs. S8–9), demonstrating excellent stability and reusability of the CNPB heterojunction composite.

In order to explore the mechanism on the visible-light driven degradation of SMX by CNPB, experiments on free radicals trapping were conducted to track the active species. h+,
O2 − and
OH are normally the main active oxidant species involved in photocatalytic processes [2,12,15,37]. Therefore, EDTA-2Na, BQ and TBA were used as the scavengers to monitor h+,
O2 − and
OH, respectively [2,6,37], and the results are shown in Fig. 10 (a). It was observed that the degradation ratio of SMX declined from 99.8% to 27% or 20%, respectively, when EDTA-2Na or BQ, respectively, was added in the reaction solution, suggesting that both h+ and
O2 − were the main reactive species responsible for the degradation of SMX. When the reaction was conducted in a solution with N2 purging to expel O2, a similar result as that of BQ was obtained, confirming again that
O2 − was involved as a prominent reactive species. However, SMX degradation ratio decreased only about 8.5% when TBA was added into the reaction solution, suggesting
OH played a minor role. The presence of
OH might be due to the synergistic effect from the reaction of h+ [37].

The photo-induced generation of
O2 − and
OH was also confirmed by ESR technique using DMPO as the trapping agent and the results are shown in Fig. 10 (b) and Fig. 10 (c). In order to avoid the overlap of ESR signals from DMPO-
O2 − with DMPO-
OH, and also impede the disproportionation of
O2 − in water which affected the reaction between
O2 − and DMPO [58,59], Ethanol rather than H2O was used as the solvent in the trapping of
O2 −, because
OH would be quenched by excessive EtOH and no signals of DMPO-
OH would appear [59]. It was obvious that no signals of DMPO-O2 − and DMPO-
OH adducts were detected in the dark, implying that O2 − and
OH could not be generated without light irradiation. After visible light irradiation for 5 min using CNPB as the photacatalyst, the ESR signals with six characteristic peaks of DMPO-O2 − appeared [12,15,37], confirming that O2 − was generated by CNPB. ESR signals with the characteristic peak intensities of 1:2:2:1 for DMPO-
OH were observed in water solution, confirming that
OH radicals were generated. Fig. 10(b) showed that the signals of DMPO-O2 − were also detected using g-C3N4 as the photocatalyst, while no signals of DMPO-O2 − were found on using BiOBr. In contrary, DMPO-
OH signals were observed with the catalysis of BiOBr, while no signals of DMPO-
OH were detected under g-C3N4 (Fig. 10(c)). Nevertheless, the intensities of ESR signals for DMPO-O2 − and DMPO-
OH generated under g-C3N4 and BiOBr, respectively, were much lower than that using CNPB. Therefore, the ESR study proved the generation of O2 − and
OH during the photocatalysis processes by CNPB, consistent with the radical quenching experiments. Besides, the amounts of radicals produced under the use of CNPB were much larger than that using the single component of g-C3N4 or BiOBr, which was also in accordance with the corresponding photocatalytic activities.

The relative band energy levels of two constructing components in a heterojunction are vital to the photocatalyst’s performance as they would determine the charge transmission direction and reactivity of photo-induced active species [12,21].The band edge potentials of VB and CB for g-C3N4 and BiOBr could be calculated according to the Eqs. (2) and (3) [12,15].
(2)
E VB
= X - -
E e
+
0.5
E g
(3)
E CB
=
E VB
-
E g

The X represents the electronegativity of the semiconductors and the corresponding values for g-C3N4 and BiOBr are 4.82 and 6.17 eV, respectively [12,37]. E e relates to free electrons energy in hydrogen scale (E e = 4.5 eV), while E g represents the semiconductor’s band gap energy. The results of optical properties in Fig. 6(b) have demonstrated that the E g values for g-C3N4 and BiOBr were 2.65 eV and 2.78 eV, respectively. Therefore, the E VB and E CB for g-C3N4 were calculated as 1.65 eV and −1.0 eV, respectively. Whereas the calculated values of E VB and E CB for BiOBr were 3.06 eV and 0.28 eV, respectively. These values are in accordance with those reported for g-C3N4 and BiOBr in literature [12,15,21].

Mott-Schottky (M−S) plots (Fig. S10) were also measured to determine the CB potential of g-C3N4 and BiOBr. The M−S curves exhibited a positive slope and indicated that both g-C3N4 and BiOBr are n-type semiconductors [60]. The flat band potential (Efb ) of g-C3N4 and BiOBr were found to be −0.88 eV and 0.36 eV. The measured Efb in M−S curve is generally regarded as Fermi level (Ef ) of the semiconductor. Since E CB in many n-type semiconductors is more negative by about 0.1 eV than the Efb
[25], thus, E CB of g-C3N4 and BiOBr could be calculated to −0.98 and 0.26 eV, respectively, which were close to the values of −1.0 eV and 0.28 eV, respectively, for g-C3N4 and BiOBr obtained from the combination of DRS spectra and Eqs. (1)–(3).

g-C3N4 and BiOBr components in the CNPB heterojunction could be excited to generate electron-hole pairs under the visible light irradiation. When the traditional heterojunction-type mechanism as illustrated in Fig. 11 (a) was employed to explain the photo-degradation of SMX by CNPB, the photo-excited electrons in the CB of g-C3N4 transported to the CB of BiOBr via the electron transfer mediator PDA [14,28,31,32,35,61], while the holes in the VB of BiOBr migrated to the VB of g-C3N4 in the same time. The charge carrier separation was thus achieved with electrons being accumulated on the CB of BiOBr while the holes being concentrated on the VB of g-C3N4. Consequently, no reactive species of
O2 − could be produced from the adsorbed O2 by the reduction of electrons in the CB of BiOBr, since the CB potential of BiOBr (0.28 eV) was higher than the reduction potential of O2/
O2 − redox pair (-0.33 V) [12,15]. However, this was in contradiction to the results obtained from the experiments on reactive species quenching and ESR study, which manifested that
O2 − was one of the main reactive species responsible for the photo-degradation of SMX. Therefore, the traditional heterojunction mechanism was not reasonable, and a Z-scheme heterojunction structure mechanism was thus elaborated in Fig. 11(b) with the main reactions illustrated in Eqs. (4)–(7) [7]. In the Z-scheme structure, the photo-induced electrons in the CB of BiOBr transported via PDA to the VB of g-C3N4 and combined with the holes, resulting in more electrons and holes accumulated in the CB of g-C3N4 and VB of BiOBr, respectively. The holes concentrated in the VB of BiOBr displayed much higher potential than that in the VB of g-C3N4, and they were able to oxidize SMX directly [12]. Because the CB potential of g-C3N4 (-1.0 eV) was more negative than that of the O2/
O2 − redox pair (-0.33 V), the electrons concentrated in the CB of g-C3N4 could reduce O2 to generate
O2 − reactive radicals. Since the VB of BiOBr possessed a higher potential (3.06 eV) than the reduction potential of OH−/
OH (1.99 eV) [12,15] and H2O/
OH (2.27 eV) [13,15],
OH might also be produced from the oxidation of H2O and/or OH− by the holes in the VB of BiOBr [15]. Therefore,
O2 −, h+, and
OH could be generated from this Z-scheme heterojunction system to oxidize SMX molecules into degraded intermediates or completely mineralize them into CO2 and H2O. This result was consistent with the conclusions obtained from the reactive species trapping experiments and ESR study. Therefore, the aforementioned discussion and experimental results confirmed that CNPB was a Z-scheme structure heterojunction photocatalyst employing PDA as efficient electron transfer mediators in promoting the separation of the photo-generated carriers and thus enhancing the photocatalyst’s redox capability.
(4) g-C3N4@PDA/BiOBr + hv → g-C3N4@PDA/BiOBr (h+ + e−)
(5) e− + O2 →
O2 −
(6) h+ + H2O/OH− →
OH
(7) h+/
O2 −/
OH + SMX → intermediates → […many steps…] → CO2 + H2O + byproducts

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of the degradation intermediates and reaction pathways
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to further explore the photodegradation processes of SMX by CNPB photocatalyst, UPLC-MS was employed to determine the reaction intermediates. Four main intermediates were found and they were named as A1-A4, respectively, with their mass spectra presented in Figs. S12–S15. Based on the detected intermediates along with the fragment’s m/z ratio, four possible pathways of the SMX degradation were proposed in Fig. 12 . One possible pathway was destruction of S-N bond in SMX by radicals attack to produce 3-amino-5-methylisoxazole (A1) along with sulfanilic acid, which was frequently found in the degradation of SMX [2,5,6]. The formation of intermediate of A2 was assumed to be originated from hydroxylation of SMX in the presence of
OH [2,5,6]. Whereas it was not the main pathway, as hydroxyl radicals were not the dominated reactive species for SMX degradation [2]. However,
OH might also come from the hydrolysis of
O2 − [7]. Another degradation pathway might be the oxidation and nitration of amino group to afford A3, which was a common intermediate produced during the advanced oxidative degradation of SMX [40]. The production of all these three intermediates were similar to the degradation of SMX by other g-C3N4-based photocatalysts, wherein the h+ and
O2 − were the main reactive species while
OH contributed a small function [2]. Apart from these three common intermediate products detected in the positive mode mass spectra, A4 was also found in the negative mode mass spectrum. It might be derived from the cleavage of S-C bond in SMX under the attack of photo-induced reactive species. Similar intermediate of A4 was also found for the visible-light photodegradation of SMX by WO3/TiO2 composite or UV-light photodegradation of SMX by permanganate [5,40]. As illustrated in Fig. 12, along with the reactions proceeding, the intermediates could be further oxidized to produce aliphatic compounds, and the benzene ring or isoxazole ring could be destructed to yield various lower molecular weight fragments [37]. Finally, some of the intermediates could be further degraded and even be completely oxidized to CO2 and H2O providing sufficient reaction time was given [1,2,37,40].

We have reported the facile construction and characterization of a novel Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst CNPB which showed high activity in degrading SMX under visible light. SMX at a concentration of 2.5 ppm could be nearly completely degraded in 60 mins under CNPB, while the degradation of SMX under g-C3N4, BiOBr, g-C3N4/BiOBr heterojunction, and the “g-C3N4@PDA + BiOBr” mixture were much lower. Investigation on the influence of reaction matrices indicated that CNPB was generally robust in the pH range of 4–8, as also for the interference of 5 mg/L HA and 5 mM HCO3 −, respectively. The enhanced efficiency of CNPB was sourced from the Z-scheme structure using PDA as efficient electron transfer mediators bridging the semiconductors of g-C3N4 and BiOBr and promoting the transport and separation of photo-generated electrons and holes. The Z-scheme structure mechanism was confirmed through the radical quenching and ESR signals trapping experiments, which manifested that the h+ and
O2 − were the major reactive species for oxidizing SMX. The degradation mechanism, main intermediates and reaction pathways were further proposed. The CNPB photocatalyst also exhibited good stability and reusability. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on construction of a Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst using the biomimetic PDA as electron transfer mediators bridging g-C3N4 and BiOBr. It may offer a sustainable and environmentally friendly way for organic pollutants treatment.
